text: At equal L2 budget, targeted noise along the inter-class axis degrades nearest-centroid accuracy far more than random isotropic noise (37.3 vs 0.5 accuracy points at eps=1.0, d=32).
The synthetic testbed reproduces closed-form predictions (clean Phi(1), targeted Phi(1-eps)), so measured gaps are attributable to geometry, not harness artifacts.

A controlled synthetic testbed (two-Gaussian classes, nearest-centroid) where clean, random-noise, and targeted-noise accuracies have closed-form predictions, validating the harness before trusting it on learned models.
Measured accuracy-vs-eps curves for random vs targeted injection at equal L2 budget, quantifying the gap.
The targeted-vs-random gap grows with dimension d (random effective shift ~ eps/sqrt(d)).
The same protocol holds on a learned model (logistic regression -> small MLP) where the targeted direction is estimated from the model's own weights.
tsest
Published Blender-to-v2e pipelines feed low-frame-rate rendered frames to v2e and rely on SuperSloMo frame interpolation to increase temporal resolution.

Frame interpolation cannot fully recover event fidelity lost to low frame rates: downstream task scores drop sharply once inter-frame displacement grows, and learned interpolation recovers only part of the loss.
ESIM couples the event simulator to the rendering engine and adaptively chooses the next render time so that the maximum expected brightness change or pixel displacement between samples is bounded.
Renderer-native event simulation computes events from the linear HDR color buffer without gamma correction, which is linear in light intensity and matches the response of real event-camera hardware.

A physics-grounded event-camera plugin exists for NVIDIA Isaac Sim that generates events inside the simulator in real time using motion-vector-based frame interpolation between sparse rendered keyframes.
Monte Carlo path-tracing noise causes spurious events; statistical tests over log-luminance samples can distinguish genuine brightness changes from rendering sampling noise.

Networks trained only on simulator-generated DVS data transfer poorly to real event cameras, attributed to event sparsity, missing noise realism, and temporal sampling uniformity of the synthetic streams.
Sensor-realistic event simulators model stochastic voltage dynamics and circuit-level noise of the DVS pixel, and networks trained on such data generalize favorably on real events.

Our snow simulation renders at 1250 fps and converts frames to events with v2e; per-frame particle evaluation makes rendering CPU-bound, and the AI denoiser must be disabled for the event pass because it fabricates brightness changes that become spurious events.

A Blender-native event renderer that consumes linear HDR radiance with adaptive temporal sampling improves synthetic event fidelity and downstream transfer at equal or lower rendering cost than 8-bit post-hoc video-to-event pipelines.
An open-source native Blender plugin that generates event streams during rendering from HDR linear radiance, emulator-agnostic across v2e, DVS-Voltmeter, and ESIM back-ends.
The first controlled ablation of rendering bit depth and quantization on synthetic event fidelity, measured through downstream perception rather than reconstruction scores.
A quantified accuracy-versus-cost trade-off of motion-vector frame synthesis against brute-force high-rate rendering for particle-dominated scenes.
